plan.contextChunk:
how light interacts with surfaces and how those interactions are
recorded with a camera.

## Light Interacting with Surfaces {#sec-light_interacting_with_surfaces}

Visible light is electromagnetic radiation, exhibiting wave effects like
diffraction. For many imaging models, however, it is helpful to
introduce the abstraction of a **light ray**, describing the light
radiation heading in a particular direction from a particular location
in space (@fig-lightSpray). A light ray is specified by its position, direction, and
intensity as a function of wavelength and polarization. In this
treatment, we ignore diffraction effects.

![A light ray from the sun strikes a surface and generates outgoing rays
of intensity and color depending on the angles of the incoming and
outgoing rays relative to the surface orientation.](figures/imaging/brdf.png){#fig-lightSpray width="75%"}

Let an incident light ray be from direction $\mathbf{p}$ and of power,
$\ell_{\texttt{in}}(\lambda)$, as a function of spectral wavelength $\lambda$ ().
The power of the outgoing light, reflected in the direction,
$\mathbf{q}$, is determined by what is called the **bidirectional
reflection distribution function** (BRDF), $F$, of the surface. If the
surface normal is $\mathbf{n}$, then outgoing power is some function,
$F$, of the surface normal, the incoming and outgoing ray directions,
the wavelength, and the incoming light power:
$$\ell_{\texttt{out}} = F \left( \ell_{\texttt{in}}, \mathbf{n}, \lambda, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q} \right)
$$ {#eq-Lambert}

### Lambertian Surfaces

In general, BRDFs can be quite complicated (see @Matusik2002),
describing both diffuse and specular components of reflection. Even
surfaces with completely diffuse reflections can give complicated
reflectance distributions @Oren1994. A useful approximation describing
diffuse reflection is the **Lambertian model**, with a particularly
simple BRDF, which we denote as $F_L$. The outgoing ray intensity,
$\ell_{\texttt{out}}$, is a function only of the surface orientation relative to the
incoming and outgoing ray directions, the wavelength, a scalar surface
reflectance, and the incoming light power:
$$
\ell_{\texttt{out}} = F_{L} \left( \ell_{\texttt{in}} (\lambda), \mathbf{n}, \mathbf{p} \right)  = a \ell_{\texttt{in}}(\lambda) \left( \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{p} \right),
$${#eq-lambert}

where $a$ is the surface **reflectance**, or
**albedo**, $\mathbf{n}$ is the surface normal vector, and $\mathbf{p}$
points toward the source of the incident light. Note that the brightness
of the outgoing light ray depends on the orientation of the surface
relative to the incident ray, as well as the reflectance, $a$, of the
surface. For a Lambertian surface, the intensity of the reflected light
is a function of the direction of the incoming light ray, but not a
function of the outgoing direction of the ray, $\mathbf{q}$.

elements: Rectangular planar surface (bright green)
Incident light ray (colored yellow to red, labeled l_in(λ), direction p)
Surface normal vector n (black arrow from surface, labeled n)
Incident direction vector p (black arrow, labeled p, aligned with incoming ray)
Outgoing direction vectors q (multiple green arrows, one black and thick, one brighter labeled q, others faded, showing a spray in a hemisphere)
Small ring or point marking position on the surface where vectors meet
Formula text floating above surface ('l_out = F(l_in, n, λ, p, q)')

interactions.label: Incident angle (°)
interactions.teaches: Shows how changing the angle of incoming light affects the dot product between the light and the surface normal.

interactions.label: Wavelength (nm)
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates how the color of the incident light changes with wavelength, making l_in(λ) visible as colored light.

interactions.label: Surface albedo
interactions.teaches: Varying albedo reveals how a more or less reflective surface modifies the brightness of outgoing rays.

interactions.label: Lambertian model
interactions.teaches: Toggles between the general BRDF formula (outgoing ray direction matters) and the Lambertian simplification (outgoing direction does not matter).

demo_steps.title: Incoming Light Perpendicular
demo_steps.narration: Let's start with the incident light straight above the surface (0° angle). The outgoing rays are brightest, as the dot product between the normal and incoming direction is maximal. Notice the outgoing ray spray is symmetrical.

demo_steps.title: Increase Incident Angle
demo_steps.narration: Now as we increase the incident angle, the incoming light becomes more slanted. Watch how the outgoing rays fade in brightness—this is because the effective illuminated area shrinks, and the dot product gets smaller.

demo_steps.title: Change Wavelength (Color)
demo_steps.narration: If we change the wavelength, notice how the color of the incident ray and the reflected rays shift across the visible spectrum. This models how different colors of light interact with the surface.

demo_steps.title: Vary Surface Albedo
demo_steps.narration: Now, let's decrease the surface albedo. Watch the outgoing rays dim—this controls the reflectivity of the surface. Higher albedo means more reflected light.

demo_steps.title: Switch to General BRDF
demo_steps.narration: When we turn off the Lambertian model, the outgoing rays now vary with direction, indicating that the outgoing direction q matters—a more general BRDF. This is essential for modeling shiny or complex surfaces.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Oblique top-down angle, looking at the surface with mild perspective so that the orientation of the rays and the plane's normal are clear. Zoom so that all rays and surface fit comfortably.
interactionPlan.notes: Use arrows with color gradients to represent incident and varied outgoing directions. Label p, n, and q clearly and animate the intensity of outgoing rays according to dot product and albedo. For λ, smoothly interpolate ray color. For showLambertian=false, modulate outgoing rays based on q direction (e.g., highlight a single outgoing ray).

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The outgoing spray arrows are all the same color (green-ish) rather than a range of green to darker greens as in the source.
The l_out formula label is different in both notation and placement; in the screenshot, it appears more cluttered and central versus large and far right in the original.
The green surface in the generated figure appears more saturated and less shaded than in the source.
The outgoing direction q and vector n overlap more tightly in the generated version, causing some slight ambiguity in vector separation.
Some arrows, such as the outgoing light spray, lack the fuller density and strong semi-transparent overlay seen in the source.
evaluation.notes: Strong interactive model closely matches the original, though color, formula label, and a few layout/clarity details differ slightly.
action_items: Adjust the color, size, and placement of the formula label to match the large, right-anchored style of the source.
Increase variety and shading/opacity in the outgoing spray arrows to better reflect the visual gradient in the original.
Slightly spread out n, p, and q vectors at their bases to avoid visual overlap and enhance clarity.
Tweak green surface brightness/saturation for a closer match to the original's pigment.
Ensure the outgoing labels and vector color (e.g., l_out) are consistent in both color and font styling to the source.

feedback.rationale: All action items involve adjustments to colors, labels, shading, placement, and layout—indicating the plan is sound but the visual execution needs refinement.
action_items: Adjust the color, size, and placement of the formula label to match the large, right-anchored style of the source.

Remove (or optionally toggle) the explanatory Lambertian/BRDF text so the default more closely matches the original figure.
Optionally hide or de-emphasize the arrow shadows beneath the arrows for closer fidelity to the source.
